Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Melanie Klein

1882 – 1960

Internal objects, phantasy, and the earliest structure of mental life.

Melanie Klein did not present herself as a philosopher of mind. She wrote as a psychoanalyst, especially as a child analyst, and her concepts emerged from clinical work with children, play, dreams, anxiety, transference, and unconscious phantasy. Yet a powerful philosophy of mind can be extracted from her work. Its central claim is this: the mind is not first of all a rational spectator of the world, nor a neutral container of representations, nor merely a linguistic system. It is an embodied, affective, conflictual, object-related inner world, structured from the beginning by unconscious phantasies of loving, attacking, losing, repairing, incorporating, expelling, protecting, and being persecuted.

In a Kleinian register, “soul” is best understood not as an immortal metaphysical substance, but as psychical life: the living, dramatic, morally charged interior of the person. This is close to the German psychoanalytic sense of Seele and to what some Russian commentators note is lost when English mind is translated too narrowly as “reason” or “consciousness”; the Kleinian “mind” is closer to psyche or soul than to mere cognition. A Russian discussion of Klein explicitly notes this translation problem, arguing that mind in Klein’s orbit is better rendered as душа or психика than as only ум, разум, or сознание.

Klein’s philosophy of mind is therefore a philosophy of the soul as an internal object-world. Consciousness is not the foundation of mind; it is a later and unstable achievement of integration, symbolization, and toleration of ambivalence. The unconscious is not an accidental lower region of the already-formed adult person; it is the primordial mode of psychic life. It is populated by internal objects, shaped by unconscious phantasy, and organized by positions of splitting and integration. Klein’s official theoretical reception rightly emphasizes the paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions, unconscious phantasy, internal objects, envy, gratitude, reparation, and the early superego as core concepts.

The theological importance of Klein lies here: her thought gives a theory of guilt, love, envy, gratitude, inner persecution, reparation, forgiveness, and the internal good object. These are not marginal religious topics; they are central to her model of psychic life. Klein was not a theologian, and some interpreters report that she was largely indifferent to religion and philosophy as explicit disciplines. But her categories are unusually fertile for theological anthropology, especially where theology concerns sin, grace, gratitude, atonement, idolatry, maternal imagery, inner persecution, and the restoration of damaged love.

2. Method: how Klein gets a theory of mind from child analysis

Klein’s theory arose from the clinical claim that children’s play can function like adult free association. Children, through toys, drawings, games, bodily gestures, and repeated dramatic scenarios, communicate unconscious conflict, anxiety, aggression, love, and phantasy. The Melanie Klein Trust describes her child technique as treating play and drawing as symbolic communication of inner dramas; she interpreted the deepest anxieties, including both positive and negative transference, rather than limiting herself to educational or supportive intervention.

This point matters philosophically. Klein’s theory of mind begins from expressive action, not from introspective certainty. The mind is known through symbolic enactment: play, dream, symptom, affect, transference, bodily complaint, fantasy, aggression, inhibition, and repetition. The subject often does not know what it is doing, but its doing has psychic form. This gives Klein a hermeneutic ontology of mind: mental life is meaningful before it is conscious.

Klein’s approach was controversial precisely because it implied that very young children already possess a rich unconscious psychic life. The British Psychoanalytical Society’s history of the “Controversial Discussions” records the conflict between Kleinians and Anna Freudians in the early 1940s, with questions about child analysis, phantasy, early ego formation, and transference at stake. A Spanish-language historical account similarly emphasizes that Klein’s work should not be seen as a simple continuation of Freud, but as a radical transformation of psychoanalytic metapsychology; it highlights the dispute with Anna Freud over whether the child can be fully analyzed and whether transference is already operative in children.

So the first philosophical move is methodological: Klein refuses to make reflective consciousness the criterion of mind. Psychic life is inferred from pattern, repetition, affect, symbol, defense, and object relation. Consciousness is one psychic achievement among others, not the essence of the mental.

3. Basic ontology: the mind as an internal object-world

Klein’s most decisive innovation is that the mind is not an empty ego confronting external reality. From the beginning, the ego is related to objects. The infant’s world is not merely a sensory manifold; it is a world of good and bad, feeding and starving, loving and attacking, protecting and persecuting presences. The “object” in object-relations theory does not simply mean an external thing. It can be a breast, a body, a mother, a father, a combined parental figure, an internal image, a part-object, a whole object, or a phantasied figure invested with love, hate, fear, and desire.

The Melanie Klein Trust defines internal objects as mental and emotional images of external objects taken into the self and colored by projection. These internal objects interact throughout life with real-world objects through projection and introjection. In early life they may be experienced concretely as located within the body; their condition is crucial to mental health because damaged, dead, or persecutory internal objects can produce anxiety and disintegration, while a stable good internal object helps the ego cohere.

This is a striking metaphysics of the psyche. The mind is a populated interior. It is not reducible to propositions, beliefs, or private sensations. Its inhabitants are not merely images in the weak sense. They function as affective presences. The infant does not just have a belief that mother feeds; the infant phantasies a good breast that nourishes, a bad breast that persecutes, a maternal body that contains treasures, rivals, babies, penises, and dangers. Klein’s theory of the Oedipus complex places these early phantasies at the root of later desire, rivalry, guilt, and superego formation.

A French account of object relation and infantile experience stresses an important point: for Klein and Susan Isaacs, drives do not merely aim at pre-given part-objects; they help create those part-objects in phantasy. The internal object is not the real external object itself, but an image or phantasy-content through which the external object is lived. This prevents a simplistic reading of Klein. She is not saying that the baby literally perceives metaphysical demons in the breast. She is saying that bodily need, affect, drive, and object relation are organized as phantasied object experience.

The philosophical formulation would be: mind is an affectively charged internal world generated by the interplay of bodily excitation, instinct, perception, projection, introjection, and object relation. It is neither merely biological nor merely representational. It is psychosomatic and relational from the start.

4. Unconscious phantasy: the elementary form of mental life

The central Kleinian concept for a philosophy of mind is unconscious phantasy. In ordinary English, “fantasy” can mean daydream, fiction, or conscious imagining. Klein’s “phantasy,” following British psychoanalytic usage, means something deeper: an unconscious mental representation of instinctual life. The Melanie Klein Trust describes unconscious phantasies as underlying every mental process and accompanying all mental activity. They are mental representations of somatic events and instincts; physical sensations are interpreted as relations with objects that cause those sensations.

Klein’s 1959 paper “Our Adult World and Its Roots in Infancy” is especially valuable here. She endorses Susan Isaacs’s formulation that phantasy is the psychic representative or corollary of instinct. She insists that unconscious phantasies are not merely daydreams, but deep unconscious activities accompanying every infantile impulse, continuing throughout development, and influencing art, scientific work, and everyday life.

This yields a radical thesis: all mental life is phantasy-mediated. There is no pure perception first, then later fantasy added. Rather, perception is already affectively interpreted. The hungry infant does not experience “neutral lack of calories.” Hunger is lived as attack, deprivation, badness, or persecution. Warmth and feeding are lived as goodness, love, protection, or union. Klein writes in “Our Adult World” that the newborn’s discomfort can be felt as coming from hostile forces, while feeding, warmth, and holding give rise to feelings associated with good forces and the first loving relation.

Portuguese-language discussions of Klein are helpful on this point. One article on unconscious phantasy emphasizes the Kleinian distinction between ordinary fantasy and unconscious phantasy, describing phantasy as the psychic representative of instinct and as an innate matrix involved in sensory perception. It also notes that phantasy helps determine the child’s attitude toward objects and functions in the formation of personality through introjection and projection. Another Portuguese account, comparing Klein and Lacan, similarly presents Kleinian phantasy as instinct’s representative and as a matrix in perception, while contrasting Klein’s emphasis on the imaginary with Lacan’s emphasis on the symbolic.

An Italian reflection makes the point even more strongly: in the Kleinian framework, phantasy accompanies every psychic process, every affective movement, every mental operation, and every somatic tension. It describes the infant’s first reality as wholly phantasy-laden and argues that Klein’s unconscious is immediately a system of objects, not merely a reservoir of drives.

So, philosophically, unconscious phantasy is not a secondary mental decoration. It is the primitive syntax of the psyche. It organizes sensation into object-relations: something gives, withholds, attacks, is attacked, enters, is expelled, is loved, is hated, is saved, is destroyed, is restored.

5. Embodiment: the mind begins in bodily experience, but bodily experience is already meaningful

Klein is not a Cartesian. The mind does not float above the body. But she is also not a crude reductionist. Bodily sensations become psychic events through phantasy. Hunger, satiation, warmth, cold, pain, holding, evacuation, sucking, biting, and bodily tension are interpreted as relations with objects.

The Melanie Klein Trust’s account of unconscious phantasy is explicit that phantasies are mental representations of somatic events and that physical sensations are interpreted as relationships with objects. Klein’s “Our Adult World” likewise links earliest anxiety to birth trauma, hunger, frustration, and bodily discomfort, while also describing the infant’s experience as directed toward good and hostile forces.

This makes Klein’s philosophy of mind deeply embodied. The body is not merely the vehicle of the mind; it is the first field of meaning. But meaning is not first linguistic. It is oral, tactile, visceral, aggressive, libidinal, and affective. The infantile psyche is organized around taking in and expelling, sucking and biting, preserving and spoiling, being filled and being emptied.

Theologically, this is important because Klein’s psyche is never “pure spirit.” Gratitude, envy, love, guilt, and reparation begin in the bodily drama of receiving nourishment from an other. Her rare explicit religious comment in “Our Adult World” is attached to eating: she reflects on the Christian grace before meals, “For what we are about to receive,” and interprets such thanksgiving as a way of being freed from resentment and envy. In Kleinian terms, gratitude is not an abstract virtue. It is a psychic capacity rooted in the ability to receive goodness without needing to spoil the source from which it comes.

6. Ego, self, and the problem of beginning

Klein departs from any developmental picture in which the infant is psychologically empty or objectless. In “Our Adult World,” she defines the self as the whole personality, including ego and id, and she states that the ego exists and operates from birth. Its earliest task is to defend against anxiety, using mechanisms such as introjection, projection, and splitting.

This does not mean that Klein imagines a mature rational ego in the newborn. The early ego is rudimentary, fragile, and unintegrated. The Melanie Klein Trust’s account of the paranoid-schizoid position describes a mental constellation present from the earliest months, marked by a rudimentary ego, persecutory anxiety, splitting, projection, introjection, idealization, and the division of self and object into good and bad parts.

Klein’s self is therefore not a given unity. It is an achievement. The ego must integrate love and hate, good and bad, self and object, internal and external. The stable good object is crucial because the ego develops around it. Klein writes that the introjected mother becomes the first good object and that the ego’s development around this good object contributes to stability, sympathy, and friendly feeling.

The philosophy of selfhood here is neither atomistic nor purely social. The self is internally relational. Its coherence depends on the condition of its inner objects. A person may appear externally unified while internally divided among persecutors, damaged loved objects, idealized protectors, hated rivals, deadened figures, and fragile reparative hopes. The soul is not a point; it is a drama seeking integration.

7. Projection and introjection: the basic operations of psychic world-making

Projection and introjection are the two great movements of Kleinian mind. Introjection takes the object into the self; projection expels parts of the self into the object. These are not merely cognitive mistakes. They are primitive operations through which the infant builds an inner world and interprets the outer world.

Klein’s “Our Adult World” presents introjection and projection as beginning in postnatal life. The outer world is taken into the self, while feelings and impulses are attributed to others. The inner world is partly built as a reflection of the external world, but adult reality judgment is never free from the influence of internal reality.

This is one of Klein’s most philosophically important claims. Reality judgment is not the simple comparison of inner representation with outer fact. It is always mediated by an internal object-world. We perceive others partly through what we have projected into them and through what we have introjected from previous relations. No adult simply sees “the other” in a neutral way. The other is encountered through love, fear, envy, suspicion, idealization, guilt, memory, and internalized object-history.

Projection also has a positive side. In “Our Adult World,” Klein says projection can make empathy possible by allowing one to put oneself into another person’s position, while hostile projection impairs empathy. Balanced introjection and projection enrich both the inner world and external relations. This means that for Klein, the boundary between self and other is not simply a wall. It is a dynamic zone of psychic traffic.

A Portuguese study of Kleinian phantasy emphasizes that introjection and projection are central to the construction of the internal world and personality, while a French account stresses that internal objects are lived through unconscious phantasy rather than being simple copies of external things.

The philosophical result is a relational theory of intentionality. To be conscious of an object, or unconsciously related to an object, is already to be engaged in a history of projection and introjection. The mind’s “aboutness” is not only representational; it is incorporative, expulsive, defensive, libidinal, aggressive, and reparative.

8. The paranoid-schizoid position: mind under persecution and splitting

Klein’s first major organization of mind is the paranoid-schizoid position. It is not simply a chronological stage that is left behind. It is a recurring mental position that may dominate in infancy but remains available throughout life. The Melanie Klein Trust defines it as a constellation beginning in the earliest months and persisting as a possible state of mind, marked by splitting of self and object into good and bad, limited integration, persecutory anxiety, projection, introjection, idealization, and omnipotent defense.

The term “paranoid” refers to persecutory anxiety: the fear that bad objects will attack, invade, or destroy the self. “Schizoid” refers to splitting: the division of object and self into sharply separated good and bad parts. The good breast is idealized as wholly nourishing and protective; the bad breast is hated and feared as persecuting, withholding, or attacking. The infant’s destructive impulses are projected outward and return as persecutory objects.

This is not only an infantile clinical idea. It is a philosophy of primitive consciousness. Under paranoid-schizoid dominance, the world is morally and affectively polarized. The mind cannot tolerate ambivalence. Good must be kept pure; bad must be expelled. The self’s hated aspects are located in others. The other becomes demonized. The good object becomes idealized and fragile. Reality is simplified because psychic survival depends on splitting.

Many adult phenomena can be interpreted through this position: fanaticism, scapegoating, conspiracy thinking, racial or political demonization, moral absolutism, paranoid social imagination, idealization of leaders, and the inability to tolerate mixed motives. This is an extrapolation from Klein rather than a direct sociological theory, but it is supported by the way later interpreters use Kleinian positions to think about social irrationality, aggression, demonization, ambivalence, and powerlessness. For example, a German-language interview with Amy Allen discusses the usefulness of Klein’s position model for critical theory, especially in distinguishing an opponent’s stance from the person rather than simply demonizing the other.

Theologically, the paranoid-schizoid position illuminates persecutory religion: a God imagined primarily as punishing persecutor; demons used as containers for disowned aggression; heretics or outsiders treated as embodiments of evil; purity protected by violent splitting; salvation imagined as omnipotent rescue from a wholly bad world. This does not imply that religion as such is paranoid-schizoid. It means that religious symbols can be used at different psychic levels.

9. The depressive position: consciousness, conscience, and the birth of moral reality

The depressive position is Klein’s most philosophically and theologically significant concept. It begins around the middle of the first year but recurs throughout life. The infant gradually realizes that the loved and hated object are the same object. The good breast and bad breast are not two unrelated entities; they belong to one mother. Love and aggression are directed toward the same beloved object. This produces guilt, sadness, concern, and the wish to repair. The Melanie Klein Trust describes the depressive position as a mental constellation involving whole-object relation, concern for the object, guilt, sadness, the urge to repair, increased ego capacity, more realistic perception, and the ability to experience self and other as separate.

This is a profound theory of moral consciousness. The moral subject is born when the psyche can say: the one I love is also the one I have hated; the object I need is also the object I have attacked in phantasy; the other has an existence beyond my need; my destructiveness matters; I want to restore what I have damaged.

Klein’s depressive position is not depression in the ordinary clinical sense, though depressive illness may involve failures or distortions of this position. It is the psychic capacity for ambivalence. One can bear that the same person is loved and resented, good and frustrating, needed and separate. Reality becomes more realistic because splitting lessens. The world is no longer black and white. Klein’s “Our Adult World” describes this developmental movement as increasing integration, with guilt and reparative urges emerging as the early superego forms.

Theologically, this is where Klein becomes indispensable. The depressive position gives a psychological account of repentance, contrition, forgiveness, mercy, and reparation without reducing them to mere moral rules. Guilt is not only punitive; it can be creative. Klein writes that constructive activities may express the wish to restore damaged loved people and that guilt has valuable aspects because it implies a wish to repair and create. The Melanie Klein Trust likewise describes reparation as grounded in love and respect for the separate other, involving the wish to repair damaged objects; moderate guilt can engender hope and concern and is central to creativity and development.

This gives us a Kleinian account of conscience: conscience is not merely obedience to law. It is concern for the damaged beloved object. The highest form of psychic morality is not fear of punishment but reparative love.

10. Superego: from persecutory conscience to realistic moral authority

Freud classically associated the superego with the resolution of the Oedipus complex. Klein moves the superego much earlier. The Melanie Klein Trust explains that, for Klein, the superego forms from the beginning of life rather than only after the Oedipal period. The early superego is severe and persecutory, but in healthy development it becomes more realistic; pathological severity is associated with psychosis and extreme disturbance.

This shift is crucial. The earliest moral authority is not yet moral in the mature sense. It is terrifying, cruel, retaliatory, and phantastically punitive. The infant who phantasies attacking the breast, mother’s body, or internal objects may fear being attacked in return. The early superego is therefore tied to projection: my aggression returns as an accusing, biting, devouring, persecuting authority.

Mature conscience emerges only through integration and depressive concern. When the good object is more securely internalized, the superego can become less sadistic and more realistic. This is a philosophy of moral development: the soul’s moral law begins as persecutory dread and may become reparative responsibility.

The theological analogy is direct but delicate. A persecutory God can function like an archaic superego: omnipotent, punitive, retaliatory, terrifying, unable to forgive. A more depressive-position religious life can imagine divine judgment together with mercy, repair, forgiveness, and restored relation. Don Carveth’s Kleinian interpretation of Christianity argues that post-Freudian theory, including Klein, allows religion to be understood not only as regression or illusion but also as potentially therapeutic, creative, and adaptive.

11. Envy and gratitude: the deepest conflict over goodness

Klein’s theory of envy is among her most severe and controversial contributions. Envy is not merely wanting what another has. It is the angry feeling that another possesses and enjoys something desirable, together with the impulse to take it away or spoil it. The Melanie Klein Trust defines envy this way and notes that Klein sees it as operating from the beginning of life, directed at the feeding breast and later at parental sexuality; envy attacks the good object and interferes with depressive integration. Gratitude, by contrast, is an expression of love and the life instinct and functions as envy’s opposite.

Philosophically, envy is an ontological revolt against dependence. The envious subject cannot bear that goodness comes from another. To receive is to acknowledge dependence; to acknowledge dependence is to recognize that the self is not omnipotent. Envy therefore tries to spoil the source of goodness. The good object must be degraded so that dependence on it becomes unnecessary or less humiliating.

This has immediate theological significance. Grace requires the capacity to receive. But the envious soul cannot receive without resentment. Klein’s own comment on Christian grace before meals is therefore more than a casual cultural observation. She sees gratitude as a psychic antidote to envy, and the practice of thanksgiving as a way of receiving without spoiling. Carveth explicitly develops this point, interpreting religious gratitude as a possible therapy for envy.

A Kleinian theology of grace would therefore say: grace is not only divine giving; it is also the transformation of the receiver. The soul must become capable of receiving goodness without attacking its source. This is why gratitude is not sentimental in Klein. It is one of the deepest signs of psychic integration.

12. Symbol formation and consciousness: from equation to representation

Klein’s account of consciousness is inseparable from symbol formation. To become conscious in a mature sense is not simply to have inner experiences. It is to distinguish symbol from thing, phantasy from external object, internal object from actual person, and wish from reality.

The Melanie Klein Trust’s page on symbol formation describes symbols as indirect or figurative representations of significant ideas, conflicts, or wishes. Symbol formation is a developmental achievement: the child moves from concrete relating to archaic objects toward symbolic substitutes. Hanna Segal’s Kleinian distinction is especially important: in a symbolic equation, the symbol is equated with the thing symbolized; in proper symbolization, symbol and object are distinct.

This distinction gives us a Kleinian theory of consciousness. In primitive mental states, the mind does not merely represent; it concretely equates. The symbol is the thing. The word, image, toy, dream object, or ritual object is experienced as the actual object. In more integrated states, the subject can use symbols while knowing that they are symbols. This allows play, language, art, thought, and religious ritual to function creatively rather than concretely.

The Melanie Klein Trust’s page on unconscious phantasy states that psychoanalysis attempts to convert unconscious phantasy into conscious thought. But this conversion does not mean eliminating phantasy. It means modifying the relation to phantasy. Consciousness emerges when the psyche can symbolize, reflect, and tolerate ambivalence instead of simply acting out, projecting, or concretely equating.

Theological symbolism depends on this distinction. A paranoid-schizoid religious mind may concretely equate symbol and object: the ritual object, image, doctrine, or enemy becomes psychically literal in a persecutory way. A depressive-position religious mind can inhabit symbol without collapsing symbol into thing. Carveth’s Kleinian reading of Christianity makes a similar distinction between literalistic or fundamentalist religion, where phantasy and external reality are insufficiently differentiated, and more mature symbolic religion, which can tolerate paradox, representation, and the gap between image and object.

13. Projective identification: porous minds and interpersonal unconscious communication

Projective identification is one of Klein’s most influential and difficult concepts. The Melanie Klein Trust defines it as an unconscious phantasy in which aspects of the self or an internal object are split off and attributed to an external object. It may also involve behavior that evokes corresponding feelings in the other person; in another form, the subject phantasies entering the other’s mind to acquire desired aspects.

Philosophically, projective identification complicates any strict individualism about mind. Mental states are not sealed inside isolated subjects. A subject may unconsciously put unbearable feelings into another, treat the other as the container of those feelings, and induce interactional patterns in which the other begins to feel or enact what has been projected. Later Kleinian and post-Kleinian analysts develop this into a sophisticated account of countertransference and containment, but even at the Kleinian level it already implies that minds are relationally porous.

This does not require a supernatural theory of telepathy. It is a theory of unconscious interpersonal enactment. The person who cannot bear helplessness may induce helplessness in others; the person who disowns rage may experience others as enraged; the person who projects badness may live among persecutors of their own psychic making.

In theological terms, projective identification helps explain communal demonology, scapegoating, and persecutory moral worlds. A community may expel its disowned aggression into a victim group, then experience that group as genuinely threatening. Conversely, more mature religious practices may provide symbolic containers for guilt, grief, aggression, and reparation, preventing destructive projection into actual others.

14. The Oedipus complex and the combined parent: desire, knowledge, and the maternal body

Klein’s Oedipus complex differs significantly from Freud’s later, more triangular formulation. For Klein, Oedipal phantasy begins earlier and is entangled with the infant’s phantasies about the mother’s body. The Melanie Klein Trust explains that Klein’s Oedipus situation involves primitive phantasies of a combined parent figure, the maternal body containing the father’s penis and rival babies, an early superego, and depressive guilt leading to the relinquishment of Oedipal desires and reparative concern for internal parents.

This has philosophical significance for the origin of knowledge. The infant’s curiosity is not detached theoretical inquiry. It is saturated with desire, envy, aggression, jealousy, and phantasy about what is inside the mother’s body. To know is often to penetrate, possess, rob, expose, or control. Epistemology begins in object relation.

Portuguese clinical-theoretical commentary on Klein’s combined object emphasizes that the mother and non-mother may be phantasied as united in continuous violent intercourse, forming an all-powerful object that is protective and terrifying, and that such phantasies need not depend on actual perception of parental coitus. This is not an empirical claim that infants literally understand adult sexuality. It is a psychoanalytic claim about how bodily, affective, and relational excitations are organized in unconscious phantasy.

The theological implications are again substantial. The maternal body becomes an archaic site of abundance, mystery, rivalry, terror, and hidden contents. Later religious symbols of womb, earth, church, divine maternity, sacred interiority, forbidden knowledge, and hidden treasure can be interpreted through this Kleinian lens. But such interpretation must be careful: Klein does not prove theological symbols are “nothing but” infantile phantasies. She gives a way to understand how religious symbols may be psychically invested.

15. Reality testing: not the abolition of phantasy but its transformation

A common misunderstanding is that psychoanalytic maturity means escaping phantasy and seeing reality “as it is.” Klein’s view is subtler. Adult reality judgment is never free from the internal world. The issue is not whether phantasy exists, but whether phantasy is rigid, persecutory, split, and concrete, or whether it is integrated, symbolized, and open to correction by experience.

Klein says in “Our Adult World” that the inner world is partly a reflection of the external one, while reality judgment is never wholly free from the influence of internal reality. Carveth’s theological reading makes a parallel point: in a Kleinian approach, reality-testing comes through the modification of phantasy rather than the complete extirpation of phantasy.

This is a philosophy of realism without naïveté. Klein is not saying that everything is subjective. External reality matters deeply; actual maternal care, deprivation, trauma, and love affect the internal world. But the external object is always lived through phantasy. Mature mind is not phantasy-free mind. It is mind capable of revising phantasy in light of love, loss, guilt, symbolization, and external reality.

16. Consciousness reconstructed: four Kleinian levels

Klein does not give a modern analytic taxonomy of consciousness, but her work supports one.

First, there is affective-somatic sentience. This is the primitive field of hunger, warmth, pain, satisfaction, terror, rage, and need. It is already psychically meaningful because bodily sensation is interpreted as object relation.

Second, there is phantasy-consciousness, or unconscious intentionality. This is not reflective awareness, but the mind’s basic dramatic organization of experience: good breast, bad breast, attacking object, damaged object, devouring object, ideal object, internal mother, combined parent. It is “consciousness” only in the broad sense of mindedness; much of it is unconscious.

Third, there is symbolic consciousness. Here the subject begins to represent rather than concretely equate. Symbols, play, words, art, dreams, and rituals can hold phantasy without being identical to the object. This is essential for thought.

Fourth, there is depressive-reflective consciousness. This is the capacity to recognize whole objects, tolerate ambivalence, accept separateness, feel guilt without collapse, and seek reparation. It is the highest form of consciousness in Klein’s moral psychology because it includes responsibility for one’s own destructive impulses.

Thus, consciousness for Klein is not primarily luminosity or self-presence. It is integration. To become conscious is to become capable of bearing psychic truth: I love and hate the same object; I depend on what I resent; I have damaged what I love; the other exists beyond me; goodness can be received without being spoiled; loss can be mourned; repair can be attempted.

17. The mind’s temporality: positions, not discarded stages

Klein’s “positions” are often misunderstood as developmental stages that one simply outgrows. The official Kleinian account is clear that both paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions continue as possible mental states throughout life. The paranoid-schizoid position may dominate early infancy, but it is never fully given up; the depressive position is first achieved in infancy but must be repeatedly reworked.

This gives Klein a non-linear philosophy of psychic time. The past is not past. Early modes of organizing experience remain structurally available. Under stress, grief, trauma, envy, humiliation, or social crisis, the adult may regress to splitting, projection, idealization, persecutory anxiety, and omnipotent defense. Conversely, depressive integration can be regained, deepened, lost, and regained again.

In “Our Adult World,” Klein emphasizes that nothing unconscious entirely loses its influence and that adult character rests on early foundations. This does not mean determinism. It means that psychic history becomes structure. The soul is layered time.

18. Art, science, creativity, and reparation

Klein does not confine phantasy to pathology. She explicitly links unconscious phantasy with art, scientific work, and everyday life. Reparation is central here. Creative activity may be an attempt to restore, recreate, preserve, or heal damaged internal objects. The Melanie Klein Trust presents reparation as central to creativity and development, grounded in love and respect for the separate other.

A Kleinian philosophy of culture would therefore say that art and thought often arise from the depressive position. Creation is not merely expression. It is repair. The artist rebuilds damaged internal worlds; the thinker restores coherence; the scientist may transform curiosity and aggression into symbolic inquiry; the ethical actor converts guilt into care.

The theological analogue is creation as reparation and reparation as participation in restoration. The soul that has passed through guilt without being destroyed by it may become capable of making, serving, healing, and giving. Klein’s “Our Adult World” connects guilt, reparation, social service, and altruism, treating constructive activity as rooted in the wish to restore damaged loved figures.

19. Religion and theology: what Klein permits, and what she does not decide

Klein’s own writings do not offer a systematic theology. Carveth notes that Klein herself did not directly address the psychological foundations of religious experience in any sustained way, and he reports the Symingtons’ judgment that she was indifferent to religion and philosophy. But this absence should not be mistaken for irrelevance. Her thought is theologically potent because it concerns the psychic roots of love, guilt, destructiveness, gratitude, envy, reparation, and internal goodness.

A careful Kleinian theology should observe five distinctions.

First, God as metaphysical reality and God as internal object are not the same question. A psychoanalyst can study the God-representation as an internal object without deciding whether God exists. Carveth explicitly separates the psychoanalytic question of God as object-representation from the metaphysical question disputed between believers and non-believers.

Second, religion can regress or heal. Freud often emphasized religion’s regressive and illusory aspects, but Carveth argues that Kleinian and other post-Freudian developments permit recognition of creative and adaptive dimensions of religion. Religion can intensify splitting, persecution, and projection; it can also support gratitude, mourning, reparation, symbolization, and the internalization of a good object.

Third, religious maturity depends on symbolic capacity. Where symbol becomes concrete equation, religion becomes persecutory, literalistic, magical, or fanatical. Where symbol remains symbol, religious life can hold paradox and mediate unconscious phantasy without collapsing into psychosis or dogmatic violence. This distinction draws on Kleinian symbol theory and Carveth’s distinction between paranoid-schizoid and depressive-position religion.

Fourth, grace is psychologically difficult because receiving goodness awakens envy. Klein’s rare comment on grace before meals is crucial: gratitude is a defense against envy not in the sense of repression, but in the sense of transformation. A Kleinian theology of grace would ask not only “What does God give?” but “What psychic capacities allow the creature to receive without spoiling?”

Fifth, atonement and reparation are not identical, but they are structurally related. Reparation is the soul’s attempt to restore the loved object it has damaged in phantasy. Christian atonement, penitence, confession, and forgiveness can be read as symbolic structures for guilt, loss, restoration, and reconciliation. Carveth notes earlier speculative analogies between Klein and Christian themes such as original sin, atonement, and Marian imagery, while setting aside crude one-to-one reductions in favor of a more careful Kleinian interpretation of Christianity.

The most rigorous conclusion is neither “religion is only infantile phantasy” nor “Klein proves theology.” Rather: Klein shows that religious symbols operate within the same psychic economy as internal objects, guilt, envy, gratitude, persecution, reparation, and symbolization. Theology that ignores these psychic dynamics becomes anthropologically thin. Psychoanalysis that dismisses theology too quickly misses one of the richest symbolic fields in which these dynamics are worked through.

20. A systematic Kleinian philosophy of mind: extracted theses

A comprehensive philosophy of mind drawn from Klein can be stated as follows.

The mind is embodied. Its earliest materials are bodily sensations, drives, pleasures, pains, frustrations, and satisfactions. But these bodily events are psychically interpreted as relations with objects.

The mind is object-related from the beginning. There is no originally self-enclosed subject who later discovers others. The ego exists from birth in rudimentary form and is immediately involved in projection, introjection, anxiety, and relation to part-objects.

The unconscious is structured by phantasy. Unconscious phantasy is the primitive mode through which instinct, affect, bodily sensation, and object relation become meaningful. It accompanies all mental activity and continues throughout life.

The self is an integration of internal object relations. A coherent self depends on the relative stability of good internal objects and the capacity to integrate love and hate. Damaged or persecutory internal objects threaten fragmentation and anxiety.

Intentionality is affective and dramatic. Mental life is always about objects, but “aboutness” is not neutral representation. It includes wanting, attacking, devouring, preserving, expelling, repairing, idealizing, envying, and mourning.

Consciousness is an achievement of symbolization and integration. To become conscious is not simply to introspect. It is to transform unconscious phantasy into thought, distinguish symbol from object, tolerate ambivalence, and recognize whole objects.

Reality testing modifies phantasy; it does not abolish it. Adult perception and judgment remain influenced by internal reality, but healthier minds can revise phantasy through experience, love, mourning, and symbolic thought.

Morality originates in object concern. The deepest moral experience is not fear of punishment but guilt over damage to the loved object and the wish to repair.

Pathology is not merely irrational belief but failure of integration. Splitting, projection, envy, persecutory anxiety, symbolic equation, and sadistic superego structures distort the mind’s relation to self, other, and reality.

Religion is psychically ambivalent. It can express persecutory splitting, omnipotent rescue, and projected aggression; it can also support gratitude, reparation, symbolic containment, forgiveness, and the internalization of a good object.

The soul, in Kleinian terms, is therefore the embodied internal world of loved, hated, damaged, persecuting, protecting, and repairable objects. Its health consists not in purity, invulnerability, or complete rational transparency, but in the capacity to love without idealizing, hate without destroying, receive without envying, mourn without collapse, and repair without omnipotence.

21. Klein’s importance for philosophy proper

Klein can be placed in conversation with several philosophical debates.

Against Cartesianism, she denies that mind is fundamentally transparent self-consciousness. The psyche is mostly unconscious, embodied, and relational.

Against empiricism in its simplest form, she denies that perception is passive reception of neutral data. Perception is phantasy-mediated and affectively organized.

Against purely linguistic theories of mind, she locates psychic meaning before language in bodily, oral, visual, tactile, and affective phantasy. Symbolization later transforms this primitive meaning but does not create mindedness from nothing.

Against individualism, she shows that selfhood is constituted through internalized relations and that psychic states move between persons through projection, introjection, and projective identification.

Against moral rationalism, she grounds ethical life in the depressive capacity for concern, guilt, and reparation, not merely in rule-following or universal law.

Against reductive secular critiques of religion, she permits a psychologically serious account of religion as both dangerous and potentially reparative. Religious symbols may be regressive, but they may also help the soul metabolize guilt, envy, dependence, and gratitude.

Against idealized humanism, she insists on primary destructiveness. Love is never innocent of hate. Goodness is always vulnerable to envy. Moral maturity is not the absence of aggression but the capacity to take responsibility for it.

22. Limits and criticisms

A rigorous report should also mark the limits of this reconstruction.

First, Klein’s claims about infancy are not straightforward observational developmental psychology. They are psychoanalytic reconstructions from child analysis, adult analysis, play, transference, and clinical inference. Her opponents objected that she attributed too much complexity to very early infancy, and the historical controversies around child analysis show how contested these claims were.

Second, her reliance on the death instinct remains philosophically and clinically controversial. One can preserve much of her theory of splitting, projection, envy, reparation, and internal objects without accepting a literal Freudian death instinct. But Klein herself often understood persecutory anxiety and destructive impulses in relation to life and death instincts.

Third, Klein’s theory risks adultomorphism if read crudely. The infant does not possess adult concepts of mother, father, sex, guilt, and repair. Kleinian phantasy should be understood as primitive affective-object organization, not as explicit infant belief.

Fourth, Klein can be overly tragic. Her vision emphasizes anxiety, envy, aggression, persecution, guilt, and damage. Its strength is moral seriousness; its danger is that ordinary development can appear saturated with violence. The counterweight within Klein herself is gratitude, love, reparation, creativity, and the stabilizing good object.

Fifth, theological use of Klein must avoid reductionism. Psychoanalysis can interpret God-representations, religious guilt, gratitude, and ritual symbolism, but it cannot by itself settle metaphysical theology. Carveth’s careful distinction between God as internal object and God as possible external metaphysical reality is essential.

23. Final synthesis

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Melanie Klein would look like this:

The human mind is a living internal world formed from embodied relations with others. Its first language is unconscious phantasy. Its first objects are loved and hated part-objects. Its first defenses are splitting, projection, introjection, omnipotence, and idealization. Its first terrors are persecution and annihilation. Its first hopes are the good object, nourishment, protection, and union. Its moral awakening comes when the loved and hated object become one, producing guilt, concern, mourning, and the wish to repair. Its consciousness matures through symbolization: the capacity to distinguish inner from outer, symbol from thing, phantasy from reality, and self from object. Its spiritual danger is envy: the spoiling of goodness because goodness comes from another. Its spiritual achievement is gratitude: the capacity to receive, preserve, and love the source of goodness. Its ethical achievement is reparation: the desire to restore what one’s destructiveness has damaged.

Klein’s “soul” is therefore not a serene rational essence. It is a conflictual, embodied, relational, symbolic, morally burdened, and potentially reparative interior world. It is tragic because love and destructiveness are intertwined from the beginning. But it is not hopeless. The good object can be internalized. The bad object can be made less persecutory. Splitting can soften. Guilt can become concern. Symbols can replace concrete equations. Envy can be transformed by gratitude. The damaged object can be repaired, at least inwardly and partially. The self can become capable of living with others as whole, separate, loved, and not wholly possessed.

That is Klein’s deepest contribution to philosophy of mind: consciousness is not the sovereign ground of the person. The ground is unconscious, embodied, object-related phantasy. But the task of psychic life is to bring this unconscious drama into symbol, responsibility, gratitude, and repair.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
